stem: A tree has \( [[n]] \) vertices. How many edges does it have?
solution: A tree on \( [[n]] \) vertices is connected and acyclic, so it has exactly \( [[n]] - 1 = [[answer]] \) edges.
distractor_rationales: This uses the number of vertices; a tree has one fewer edge than vertices.
This adds an edge, which would create a cycle.
This removes one too many edges, disconnecting the tree.
This roughly doubles the count, as if each vertex contributed an edge.
This is off by three from the correct count.
This is off by three from the correct count.

stem: A tree has \( [[e]] \) edges. How many vertices does it have?
solution: A tree on \( n \) vertices always has \( n - 1 \) edges, so a tree with \( [[e]] \) edges has \( n = [[e]] + 1 = [[answer]] \) vertices.
distractor_rationales: This uses the number of edges directly; a tree has one MORE vertex than edges.
This removes a vertex, as if edges and vertices were equal in a tree.
This adds one too many vertices.
This roughly doubles the count, as if each edge contributed two new vertices.
This is off by three from the correct count.
This is off by three from the correct count.

stem: A connected graph has \( [[n]] \) vertices and \( [[e]] \) edges. What is the minimum number of edges that would need to be removed to turn it into a tree (equivalently, how many independent cycles does it contain)?
solution: A spanning tree of the graph has exactly \( [[n]] - 1 \) edges, and every edge beyond that creates one more independent cycle. So the number of extra (removable) edges is \( [[e]] - ([[n]] - 1) = [[e]] - [[n]] + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the \( +1 \) correction; a tree on \( n \) vertices has \( n-1 \), not \( n \), edges.
This overcorrects by using \( +2 \) instead of \( +1 \).
This swaps the roles of \( e \) and \( n \).
This uses the full edge count, as if the graph had no spanning tree at all.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
